highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.186 Released: CLI MCP Authentication & Workflow Filtering
highlights.summary: The latest Claude Code release adds `claude mcp login <name>` and `claude mcp logout <name>` commands so you can authenticate MCP servers directly from the terminal without opening the interactive menu. A `--no-browser` flag enables authentication over SSH by redirecting through stdin. The `/workflows` agent view also gains a status filter (press `f`) for easier navigation.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.183: Safer Auto Mode with Destructive Git Command Blocking
highlights.summary: Claude Code now blocks dangerous git commands like `git reset --hard`, `git clean -fd`, and `git stash drop` when running in auto mode unless you explicitly asked to discard local work. It also prevents `git commit --amend` on commits the agent didn't create this session. This is a significant safety improvement for anyone running Claude Code autonomously on real projects.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.181: Set Config from the Prompt with /config key=value
highlights.summary: You can now change Claude Code settings on the fly by typing `/config key=value` directly in the prompt (for example, `/config thinking=false`). This works in interactive mode, headless `-p` mode, and Remote Control, making it faster to tweak behavior without hunting through config files. macOS users also get an opt-in `sandbox.allowAppleEvents` setting for sandboxed commands.

highlights.title: Show HN: Recall – Local Project Memory for Claude Code
highlights.summary: Recall is an open-source tool that gives Claude Code persistent, local memory about your project so context isn't lost between sessions. It stores notes and summaries locally, meaning nothing is sent to external servers beyond your normal Claude API calls. This is especially useful for long-running projects where re-explaining architecture each session wastes tokens and time.

highlights.title: Agent Rigor – Stop Your AI Coding Assistant from Doom-Looping
highlights.summary: Agent Rigor is an open-source tool designed to detect and break the repetitive failure loops that AI coding agents can fall into when stuck on a problem. It adds guardrails that interrupt the agent when it cycles through the same failed attempts, saving both tokens and frustration. A handy addition for developers running Claude Code in agentic or automated modes.

highlights.title: From Zero to Claude Code in 19 Minutes (No Code)
highlights.summary: This beginner-friendly YouTube tutorial walks through getting started with Claude Code from scratch in under 20 minutes, requiring no prior coding experience. It covers installation, basic commands, and running your first AI-assisted task. A great starting point if you've just heard about Claude Code and want a quick, practical introduction.

highlights.title: The Text in Claude Code's Extended Thinking Output Is Not Authentic
highlights.summary: This Hacker News–linked article argues that the visible 'thinking' text shown in Claude Code's Extended Thinking mode is not a genuine transcript of the model's internal reasoning process. The piece raises questions about transparency and what developers should actually trust when interpreting Claude's reasoning outputs. Worth reading if you rely on Extended Thinking to understand or audit how Claude reaches its conclusions.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: A compact but comprehensive YouTube tutorial aimed at developers who want to move beyond the basics and use Claude Code more effectively. The video covers key workflows, practical prompting strategies, and tips for getting consistent results. Ideal for users who have already installed Claude Code and want to level up their daily usage.

tips_of_the_day: Before running Claude Code in auto mode on a feature branch, commit or stash any local work you care about — v2.1.183's new git safeguards protect you, but a clean working tree is still your best insurance.

Use `/config thinking=false` at the start of a session for quick, repetitive tasks like renaming variables or writing tests — disabling Extended Thinking speeds up responses and reduces token costs when deep reasoning isn't needed.

If you work over SSH or in a headless CI environment, authenticate your MCP servers once using `claude mcp login <name> --no-browser` so they stay authorized across sessions without requiring a browser redirect.
